solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int[] validSequence(String word1, String word2) {
        int n = word2.length();
        int m = word1.length();
        int[] result = new int[n];
        Arrays.fill(result, -1);
        int diffCount = 0;
        int j = 0;

        for (int i = 0; i < m && j < n; i++) {
            if (word1.charAt(i) == word2.charAt(j)) {
                result[j++] = i;
            } else if (diffCount < 1) {
                int[] tempResult = new int[n];
                System.arraycopy(result, 0, tempResult, 0, j);
                tempResult[j++] = i;
                diffCount++;
                int k = j;
                for (int l = i + 1; k < n && l < m; l++) {
                    if (word1.charAt(l) == word2.charAt(k)) {
                        tempResult[k++] = l;
                    }
                }
                if (k == n) {
                    if (compareLex(result, tempResult, j)) {
                        System.arraycopy(tempResult, 0, result, 0, n);
                    }
                }
                j--;
                diffCount--;
            }
        }

        if (result[n - 1] == -1) {
            return new int[0];
        }
        return result;
    }

    private boolean compareLex(int[] a, int[] b, int len) {
        for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
            if (a[i] != b[i]) {
                return a[i] < b[i];
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O(N * M)
space complexity: O(N)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution iterates through `word1` and tries to match characters with `word2`, allowing at most one character to differ. It maintains a lexicographically smallest sequence by comparing potential sequences when a difference is found. The time complexity is O(N * M) due to nested loops, and space is O(N) for storing the result. The approach is efficient for moderate input sizes but may not be optimal for very large inputs.
